name: Malaysia
overview: Malaysia's Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (PDPA) covers personal data processed in commercial transactions. The Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 — in force in phases during 2025 — added mandatory breach notification, DPO appointment, processor obligations, a right to data portability, and replaced the transfer whitelist with an adequacy/safeguards model. The Commissioner has issued supporting guidelines.

legal_instruments.title: Personal Data Protection Act 2010 (Act 709)
legal_instruments.summary: Seven data protection principles applying to personal data in commercial transactions, registration requirements for certain sectors, sensitive-data consent rules and criminal-style penalties.

legal_instruments.title: Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024
legal_instruments.summary: Phased in during 2025: mandatory breach notification, DPO appointment, direct obligations on processors, biometric data classed as sensitive, data portability right, higher penalties (up to RM 1 million), and reformed cross-border transfer test.

legal_instruments.title: Personal Data Protection Regulations 2013
legal_instruments.summary: Implementing regulations on registration of data users, security standards, retention and data integrity.

developments.title: Breach notification and DPO obligations commence
developments.summary: The final phase of the 2024 Amendment Act took effect, requiring notification of significant breaches to the Commissioner (within 72 hours) and affected individuals, and DPO appointment above processing thresholds.

developments.title: Commissioner issues breach notification and DPO guidelines
developments.summary: JPDP published guidelines operationalising the amendment's breach notification and DPO requirements ahead of commencement.

penalties: Up to RM 1 million and/or imprisonment up to 3 years for breach of core principles (post-amendment); breach-notification failure up to RM 250,000.
cross_border: Post-2024 amendment: transfers permitted to jurisdictions with substantially similar law or adequate protection; otherwise rely on consent, contract necessity or other statutory conditions. The old ministerial whitelist mechanism was abolished.
